metric_value: I recall being briefed on the Niagara roadmap circa 2000.
notes: Kastner blog comment; recollection of 2000 analyst briefing

metric_value: Sun has had a poor delivery record in SPARC over the past several years. That has hurt the company; some say it has hurt badly.
With today's big WSJ story on computer power consumption, Sun's eight cores in a 70-watt envelope is the right recipe. But wouldn't this have been market-turning, say, mid-2002?
Niagara is a big help to Sun's SPARC base, but I doubt it will shake the market's penchant for x64-based servers, in Sun's case, AMD Opterons.
UltraSPARC T1 (Niagara) servers will be available with 4, 6, or 8 cores; each core capable of 8 threads; maximum server power consumption 70 watts.
Sun pitched Niagara as recapture strategy for users who switched to low-cost Windows/Linux x86 via multithreading and power consumption advantages.
IBM circulated a poison-pen FUD gram criticizing Niagara launch timing as an attempt to buy momentum with an early product release.

metric_name: Sun acquired by Oracle; Niagara line absorbed
metric_value: Oracle acquired Sun in January 2010; SPARC T-series continued under Oracle through T8 (2017) but never reclaimed x64 market share; by 2017 Oracle laid off the Solaris/SPARC core team.
